title: Por vs Para — worked example
description: A hand-written reference pack showing every exercise type. Safe to delete once a real PDF has been ingested; the /ingest-pdf skill mirrors this shape.

lessons.summary: Use PARA for destination, purpose, deadline and recipient (the goal or endpoint). Use POR for cause/reason, exchange, duration, movement through a place, and 'on behalf of' (the motive or means). A quick test: PARA points forward to a goal; POR points back to a cause.

examples.english: I study (in order) to be a doctor.

examples.spanish: La tarea es para el lunes.

items.explanation: PARA marks the recipient — the person the gift is destined for.

items.promptEnglish: Thanks for coming to my party.
items.explanation: POR expresses the reason you are grateful — the cause of the thanks.

items.promptEnglish: I study a lot (in order) to pass the exam.
items.explanation: PARA + infinitive expresses purpose: the goal of studying.

items.promptEnglish: I paid twenty euros for this book.
items.explanation: POR marks exchange — money given in return for something.

items.prompt: Rewrite expressing a deadline: "Necesito el informe el viernes." → "Necesito el informe ___ el viernes."
items.promptEnglish: I need the report by Friday.
items.explanation: A deadline (by/for a point in time) takes PARA.

items.prompt: Escribe una frase que use "por" para expresar duración de tiempo.
items.promptEnglish: Write a sentence using "por" to express duration of time.
items.guidance: The sentence must be grammatical Spanish and use 'por' to express a length of time (e.g. 'Viví en Madrid por dos años'). Mark incorrect if it uses 'para', or uses 'por' for a non-duration meaning.
items.explanation: POR expresses duration — the span of time an action lasts.
